---
title: "Singapore Employment Rate by Education Attainment Across Age & Gender"
subtitle: "AAI1001 Team Project Proposal"
author: "AAI1001 Team 32"
date: "03/05/2026"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "Table of Contents"
    toc-location: left
    smooth-scroll: true
    embed-resources: true
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show Code"
    fig-width: 9
    fig-height: 5
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <link href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Lora:ital,wght@0,400;0,600;0,700;1,400&family=IBM+Plex+Sans:wght@300;400;500;600&family=IBM+Plex+Mono:wght@400;500&display=swap" rel="stylesheet">
        <style>
        /* ══════════════════════════════════════════
           GLOBAL TYPOGRAPHY & COLOUR SYSTEM
        ══════════════════════════════════════════ */
        :root {
          --bg: #fafaf8;
          --surface: #ffffff;
          --ink: #18181b;
          --ink2: #3f3f46;
          --muted: #71717a;
          --faint: #e4e4e7;
          --rule: #d4d4d8;
          --accent: #1e3a5f;
          --c1:#5a5a5a; --c2:#0072b2; --c3:#e69f00; --c4:#7b2d8b; --c5:#009e73;
          --male-col: #1e3a5f; --fem-col: #7c2d12;
          --male-bg: linear-gradient(to left, #ddeaf8, #eef4fc);
          --fem-bg:  linear-gradient(to right, #fce8ec, #fdf2f4);
        }
        body {
          font-family: 'IBM Plex Sans', sans-serif;
          background: var(--bg);
          color: var(--ink);
          line-height: 1.75;
          font-size: 15px;
        }
        h1, h2, h3, h4, h5 {
          font-family: 'Lora', serif;
          line-height: 1.25;
          color: var(--ink);
        }
        h1 { color: var(--accent); }
        code, pre, .sourceCode {
          font-family: 'IBM Plex Mono', monospace !important;
        }
        /* TOC font */
        #TOC { font-family: 'IBM Plex Sans', sans-serif; font-size: 13.5px; }
        #TOC a { color: var(--ink2); }
        #TOC a:hover { color: var(--accent); }

        /* ── SECTION HEADER ACCENT ── */
        .section-header { border-left: 4px solid var(--accent); padding-left: 18px; margin-bottom: 32px; }
        .section-header .section-num { font-family: 'IBM Plex Mono', monospace; font-size: .8rem; color: var(--muted); letter-spacing: .1em; text-transform: uppercase; margin-bottom: 4px; }
        .section-header p { font-size: 14px; color: var(--muted); margin-top: 6px; }

        /* ── TABLES (native Quarto + custom imp-table) ── */
        table { font-family: 'IBM Plex Sans', sans-serif; font-size: 13.5px; border-collapse: collapse; width: 100%; }
        thead tr { background: var(--accent); color: white; }
        thead th { padding: 10px 14px; text-align: left; font-weight: 500; letter-spacing: .03em; }
        tbody tr { border-bottom: 1px solid var(--faint); }
        tbody tr:last-child { border-bottom: none; }
        tbody tr:nth-child(even) { background: #f4f4f5; }
        tbody td { padding: 9px 14px; vertical-align: top; line-height: 1.5; }
        .imp-table-wrap { overflow-x: auto; margin: 20px 0 28px; border: 1px solid var(--rule); border-radius: 8px; }
        .imp-table-wrap table thead tr { background: var(--accent); color: white; }
        .imp-table-wrap table tbody td:first-child { font-family: 'IBM Plex Mono', monospace; font-weight: 500; color: var(--accent); white-space: nowrap; }

        /* ── WEAKNESS / STRENGTH BADGES ── */
        .badge {
          display: inline-block;
          font-family: 'IBM Plex Mono', monospace;
          font-size: 10.5px;
          font-weight: 600;
          letter-spacing: .04em;
          padding: 2px 7px;
          border-radius: 4px;
          white-space: nowrap;
          vertical-align: middle;
          margin: 1px 2px;
        }
        .badge-w { background: #fef3c7; color: #92400e; border: 1px solid #fde68a; }
        .badge-s { background: #d1fae5; color: #065f46; border: 1px solid #6ee7b7; }
        .badge-i { background: #ede9fe; color: #4c1d95; border: 1px solid #c4b5fd; }

        /* ── STRENGTHS PRESERVED CALLOUT ── */
        .strengths-preserved {
          background: #ecfdf5;
          border: 1px solid #6ee7b7;
          border-left: 4px solid #059669;
          border-radius: 0 8px 8px 0;
          padding: 14px 18px;
          margin: 18px 0 24px;
          font-size: 13.5px;
          line-height: 1.65;
        }
        .strengths-preserved .sp-title {
          font-family: 'Lora', serif;
          font-weight: 600;
          font-size: 14px;
          color: #065f46;
          margin-bottom: 10px;
          display: flex;
          align-items: center;
          gap: 7px;
        }
        .sp-row { display: flex; gap: 10px; align-items: flex-start; padding: 5px 0; }
        .sp-row + .sp-row { border-top: 1px solid #a7f3d0; }
        .sp-badge { font-family: 'IBM Plex Mono', monospace; font-size: 11px; font-weight: 700; color: #065f46; background: #d1fae5; border: 1px solid #6ee7b7; border-radius: 4px; padding: 1px 6px; flex-shrink: 0; margin-top: 2px; }
        .sp-text { color: #047857; font-size: 13px; }

        /* ── 3.2 JUSTIFICATION CARDS ── */
        .justify-grid { display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr; gap: 16px; margin: 20px 0 28px; }
        @media(max-width:640px) { .justify-grid { grid-template-columns: 1fr; } }
        .jcard { background: var(--surface); border: 1px solid var(--rule); border-radius: 10px; padding: 20px 18px; position: relative; overflow: hidden; }
        .jcard::before { content: ''; position: absolute; top: 0; left: 0; right: 0; height: 3px; background: var(--accent); border-radius: 10px 10px 0 0; }
        .jcard-icon { font-size: 26px; margin-bottom: 10px; display: block; }
        .jcard-title { font-family: 'Lora', serif; font-size: .95rem; font-weight: 600; color: var(--ink); margin-bottom: 7px; line-height: 1.3; }
        .jcard-body { font-size: 13px; color: var(--muted); line-height: 1.6; }
        .jcard-body strong { color: var(--ink2); }

        /* ── CUSTOM CALLOUT ── */
        .callout-custom { border-left: 3px solid var(--accent); background: #eef2f9; padding: 14px 18px; border-radius: 0 6px 6px 0; margin: 18px 0; font-size: 14px; }
        .callout-custom.insight { border-color: #059669; background: #ecfdf5; }
        .callout-custom strong { display: block; margin-bottom: 4px; font-size: 13px; letter-spacing: .04em; text-transform: uppercase; }

        /* ── DECISION LIST ── */
        .decision-list { list-style: none; margin: 12px 0 20px; padding: 0; }
        .decision-list li { display: flex; gap: 14px; padding: 12px 0; border-bottom: 1px solid var(--faint); font-size: 14px; line-height: 1.6; }
        .decision-list li:last-child { border-bottom: none; }
        .decision-num { flex-shrink: 0; width: 26px; height: 26px; background: var(--accent); color: white; border-radius: 50%; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center; font-size: 12px; font-weight: 600; margin-top: 1px; }

        /* ── DIM PILLS ── */
        .dim-grid { display: flex; flex-wrap: wrap; gap: 8px; margin: 12px 0 20px; }
        .dim-pill { display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 7px; background: var(--surface); border: 1px solid var(--rule); border-radius: 6px; padding: 6px 12px; font-size: 13px; }
        .dim-pill .dim-icon { font-size: 16px; }
        .dim-pill .dim-label { font-weight: 500; color: var(--ink2); }

        /* ── FEATURE GRID ── */
        .feature-grid { display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr; gap: 12px; margin: 16px 0 24px; }
        @media(max-width:600px) { .feature-grid { grid-template-columns: 1fr; } }
        .feature-card { background: var(--surface); border: 1px solid var(--rule); border-radius: 8px; padding: 14px 16px; }
        .feature-card .fc-title { font-weight: 600; color: var(--ink); margin-bottom: 5px; display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 7px; font-size: 13.5px; }
        .feature-card p { font-size: 13px; color: var(--muted); margin: 0; line-height: 1.5; }

        /* ═══ VIZ WRAP ═══ */
        .viz-wrap { background: var(--surface); border: 1px solid var(--rule); border-radius: 12px; padding: 0 20px 24px; margin: 24px 0; overflow: visible; }

        /* ── CONTROLS BAR ── */
        .controls-bar { display: flex; flex-wrap: wrap; gap: 10px; align-items: center; padding: 16px 0 14px; border-bottom: 1px solid var(--faint); margin-bottom: 16px; }
        .ctrl-label { font-size: 11px; font-weight: 600; color: var(--muted); text-transform: uppercase; letter-spacing: .07em; white-space: nowrap; }
        .seg-toggles { display: flex; flex-wrap: wrap; gap: 6px; }
        .seg-btn { display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 5px; border: 1px solid var(--rule); border-radius: 20px; background: white; padding: 4px 10px; font-size: 12px; font-family: 'IBM Plex Sans', sans-serif; cursor: pointer; transition: all .15s; color: var(--ink2); }
        .seg-btn .swatch { width: 10px; height: 10px; border-radius: 2px; flex-shrink: 0; }
        .seg-btn.active { border-color: transparent; color: white; }
        .seg-btn.active[data-idx="0"] { background: var(--c1); }
        .seg-btn.active[data-idx="1"] { background: var(--c2); }
        .seg-btn.active[data-idx="2"] { background: var(--c3); }
        .seg-btn.active[data-idx="3"] { background: var(--c4); }
        .seg-btn.active[data-idx="4"] { background: var(--c5); }
        .seg-btn:not(.active) { opacity: .45; }
        .highlight-select { margin-left: auto; display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 8px; position: relative; }
        .age-filter-btn { font-family: 'IBM Plex Sans', sans-serif; font-size: 12px; border: 1px solid var(--rule); border-radius: 6px; padding: 5px 12px; background: white; color: var(--ink2); cursor: pointer; white-space: nowrap; transition: border-color .15s; }
        .age-filter-btn:hover, .age-filter-btn.open { border-color: var(--accent); color: var(--accent); }
        .age-dropdown { position: absolute; top: calc(100% + 6px); right: 0; background: white; border: 1px solid var(--rule); border-radius: 8px; padding: 10px 12px; z-index: 100; box-shadow: 0 6px 24px rgba(0,0,0,.1); min-width: 220px; }
        .age-dd-header { display: flex; justify-content: space-between; align-items: center; margin-bottom: 8px; padding-bottom: 8px; border-bottom: 1px solid var(--faint); }
        .age-dd-action { font-family: 'IBM Plex Sans', sans-serif; font-size: 11px; padding: 2px 8px; border: 1px solid var(--rule); border-radius: 4px; cursor: pointer; background: white; color: var(--accent); font-weight: 500; }
        .age-dd-action:hover { background: var(--faint); }
        .age-checkbox-grid { display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr; gap: 4px 12px; }
        .age-cb-item { display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 6px; font-size: 12px; color: var(--ink2); cursor: pointer; padding: 3px 0; }
        .age-cb-item input[type=checkbox] { accent-color: var(--accent); width: 13px; height: 13px; cursor: pointer; flex-shrink: 0; }

        /* ── GENDER STRIP ── */
        .gender-strip { display: flex; margin-bottom: 6px; }
        .gs-male { flex: 1; background: #ddeaf8; border: 1px solid #b8d0ee; border-right: none; border-radius: 6px 0 0 6px; padding: 7px 14px; font-size: 12px; font-weight: 700; letter-spacing: .08em; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(--male-col); display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: flex-end; gap: 5px; }
        .gs-male::after { content: '◀'; font-size: 9px; opacity: .6; }
        .gs-age { width: 80px; flex-shrink: 0; background: #f0f0f0; border-top: 1px solid var(--rule); border-bottom: 1px solid var(--rule); text-align: center; font-size: 10px; font-weight: 600; color: #666; letter-spacing: .05em; text-transform: uppercase; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center; }
        .gs-fem { flex: 1; background: #fce8ec; border: 1px solid #f0b8c4; border-left: none; border-radius: 0 6px 6px 0; padding: 7px 14px; font-size: 12px; font-weight: 700; letter-spacing: .08em; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(--fem-col); display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 5px; }
        .gs-fem::before { content: '▶'; font-size: 9px; opacity: .6; }

        /* ── CHART ROWS ── */
        .row-group { display: flex; flex-direction: column; gap: 4px; }
        .chart-row { display: flex; align-items: center; height: 34px; position: relative; border-radius: 4px; }
        .chart-row.row-hidden { display: none; }
        .chart-row.highlighted .wing.male { background: linear-gradient(to left, #c8ddf5, #ddeaf8); }
        .chart-row.highlighted .wing.fem  { background: linear-gradient(to right, #f5c2cc, #fce8ec); }
        .wing { flex: 1; display: flex; align-items: center; height: 100%; position: relative; }
        .wing.male { justify-content: flex-end; padding-left: 44px; background: var(--male-bg); border-radius: 4px 0 0 4px; }
        .wing.fem  { justify-content: flex-start; padding-right: 44px; background: var(--fem-bg); border-radius: 0 4px 4px 0; }
        .age-col { width: 80px; flex-shrink: 0; text-align: center; font-size: 11.5px; font-weight: 600; color: #444; z-index: 4; background: #ebebeb; height: 100%; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center; border-top: 1px solid #ddd; border-bottom: 1px solid #ddd; }
        .bar-wrap { position: relative; height: 22px; display: flex; align-items: center; flex-shrink: 0; }
        .stacked { display: flex; height: 100%; border-radius: 3px; overflow: hidden; width: 100%; }
        .wing.male .stacked { flex-direction: row-reverse; border-radius: 3px 0 0 3px; }
        .wing.fem  .stacked { flex-direction: row; border-radius: 0 3px 3px 0; }
        .seg { transition: filter .15s, width .4s cubic-bezier(.22,.68,0,1.2); }
        .chart-row:hover .seg { filter: brightness(1.08); }
        .emp-tip { position: absolute; font-size: 11px; font-weight: 700; color: #fff; white-space: nowrap; pointer-events: none; top: 50%; transform: translateY(-50%); z-index: 10; background: rgba(30,40,60,0.72); padding: 2px 6px; border-radius: 4px; letter-spacing: .02em; }
        .wing.male .emp-tip { right: calc(100% + 6px); left: auto; }
        .wing.fem  .emp-tip { left: calc(100% + 6px); right: auto; }

        /* ── AXIS ── */
        .axis-strip { display: flex; margin-top: 6px; font-size: 10.5px; color: var(--muted); }
        .ax-half { flex: 1; display: flex; }
        .ax-half.male { justify-content: flex-end; }
        .ax-age-gap { width: 80px; flex-shrink: 0; }
        .tick-list { display: flex; width: 100%; justify-content: space-between; }
        .x-label { text-align: center; font-size: 11.5px; font-style: italic; color: var(--muted); margin-top: 4px; }

        /* ── LEGEND ── */
        .legend { display: flex; flex-wrap: wrap; gap: 8px 20px; justify-content: center; margin-top: 18px; }
        .leg-item { display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 6px; font-size: 12.5px; color: var(--muted); }
        .leg-swatch { width: 20px; height: 10px; border-radius: 2px; flex-shrink: 0; }
        .source { font-size: 11px; color: #a1a1aa; text-align: center; margin-top: 14px; letter-spacing: .02em; }

        /* ── TOOLTIP ── */
        .butterfly-tooltip { position: fixed; background: white; border: 1px solid var(--rule); border-radius: 10px; padding: 12px 16px; font-size: 12px; pointer-events: none; z-index: 9999; min-width: 240px; display: none; box-shadow: 0 8px 28px rgba(0,0,0,.13); }
        .tt-head { font-family: 'Lora', serif; font-size: 13.5px; margin-bottom: 8px; }
        .tt-emp { display: flex; justify-content: space-between; margin-bottom: 6px; padding-bottom: 6px; border-bottom: 1px solid var(--faint); font-size: 13px; }
        .tt-row { display: flex; justify-content: space-between; align-items: center; padding: 2.5px 0; gap: 12px; }
        .tt-lbl { display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 6px; color: var(--muted); }
        .tt-dot { width: 9px; height: 9px; border-radius: 2px; flex-shrink: 0; }
        .tt-val { font-weight: 600; color: var(--ink); font-family: 'IBM Plex Mono', monospace; font-size: 12px; }
        .tt-note { font-size: 10.5px; color: var(--muted); margin-top: 5px; padding-top: 5px; border-top: 1px solid var(--faint); }
        /* ── SECTION 5: EXTRA COLOUR TOKENS ── */
        :root {
          --green: #059669;
          --amber: #b45309;
          --red:   #b91c1c;
          --accent2: #2d6a9f;
        }

        /* ── CALLOUT COLOUR VARIANTS (green / amber) ── */
        .callout-custom.green { border-color: var(--green); background: #ecfdf5; }
        .callout-custom.amber { border-color: var(--amber); background: #fffbeb; }
        .callout-custom.green strong { color: var(--green); }
        .callout-custom.amber strong { color: var(--amber); }

        /* ── DATASET CARDS ── */
        .ds-grid { display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr; gap: 14px; margin: 20px 0 28px; }
        @media(max-width:640px) { .ds-grid { grid-template-columns: 1fr; } }
        .ds-card { background: var(--surface); border: 1px solid var(--rule); border-radius: 10px; padding: 18px 18px 14px; position: relative; overflow: hidden; }
        .ds-card::before { content: ''; position: absolute; top: 0; left: 0; right: 0; height: 3px; background: var(--accent); border-radius: 10px 10px 0 0; }
        .ds-badge { display: inline-block; font-family: 'IBM Plex Mono', monospace; font-size: 10px; font-weight: 600; letter-spacing: .08em; text-transform: uppercase; background: var(--accent); color: white; padding: 2px 8px; border-radius: 4px; margin-bottom: 8px; }
        .ds-title { font-family: 'Lora', serif; font-size: .95rem; font-weight: 600; color: var(--ink); margin-bottom: 6px; line-height: 1.35; }
        .ds-meta { font-size: 12px; color: var(--muted); line-height: 1.6; }
        .ds-meta strong { color: var(--ink2); }
        .ds-tag { display: inline-block; background: #f0f4f8; border: 1px solid var(--rule); border-radius: 4px; font-size: 11px; padding: 1px 7px; margin: 3px 2px 0 0; color: var(--ink2); font-family: 'IBM Plex Mono', monospace; }

        /* ── PIPELINE STEPS ── */
        .pipeline-wrap { margin: 20px 0 28px; }
        .pipe-step { display: flex; gap: 16px; padding: 16px 0; border-bottom: 1px solid var(--faint); }
        .pipe-step:last-child { border-bottom: none; }
        .pipe-num { flex-shrink: 0; width: 34px; height: 34px; background: var(--accent); color: white; border-radius: 50%; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center; font-size: 13px; font-weight: 700; margin-top: 1px; font-family: 'IBM Plex Mono', monospace; }
        .pipe-body { flex: 1; }
        .pipe-title { font-weight: 600; color: var(--ink); font-size: 14.5px; margin-bottom: 4px; }
        .pipe-desc { font-size: 13.5px; color: var(--ink2); line-height: 1.6; }
        .pipe-desc code { font-family: 'IBM Plex Mono', monospace; font-size: 12px; background: #f1f5f9; padding: 1px 5px; border-radius: 3px; color: var(--accent2); }
        .pipe-tags { margin-top: 7px; display: flex; flex-wrap: wrap; gap: 5px; }
        .pipe-tag { font-family: 'IBM Plex Mono', monospace; font-size: 11px; background: #eef2f9; border: 1px solid #c7d5e8; color: var(--accent); padding: 2px 8px; border-radius: 4px; }

        /* ── CHALLENGE TABLE ── */
        .challenge-table { width: 100%; border-collapse: collapse; font-size: 13.5px; margin: 16px 0 24px; border: 1px solid var(--rule); border-radius: 8px; overflow: hidden; }
        .challenge-table thead tr { background: var(--accent); color: white; }
        .challenge-table thead th { padding: 10px 14px; text-align: left; font-weight: 500; letter-spacing: .03em; }
        .challenge-table tbody tr { border-bottom: 1px solid var(--faint); }
        .challenge-table tbody tr:last-child { border-bottom: none; }
        .challenge-table tbody tr:nth-child(even) { background: #f7f8fa; }
        .challenge-table tbody td { padding: 10px 14px; vertical-align: top; line-height: 1.55; }
        .challenge-table tbody td:first-child { font-family: 'IBM Plex Mono', monospace; font-size: 12.5px; color: var(--accent2); white-space: nowrap; font-weight: 500; }
        .sev-high { color: var(--red);   font-weight: 600; }
        .sev-med  { color: var(--amber); font-weight: 600; }
        .sev-low  { color: var(--green); font-weight: 600; }

        /* ── OUTPUT SCHEMA CARDS ── */
        .schema-wrap { display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr; gap: 14px; margin: 16px 0 24px; }
        @media(max-width:600px) { .schema-wrap { grid-template-columns: 1fr; } }
        .schema-card { background: var(--surface); border: 1px solid var(--rule); border-radius: 8px; padding: 14px 16px; }
        .schema-card .sc-head { font-weight: 700; font-size: 13px; color: var(--ink); margin-bottom: 8px; border-bottom: 1px solid var(--faint); padding-bottom: 6px; font-family: 'IBM Plex Mono', monospace; }
        .schema-card .sc-row { display: flex; justify-content: space-between; padding: 3px 0; font-size: 12.5px; }
        .schema-card .sc-col { color: var(--accent2); font-family: 'IBM Plex Mono', monospace; }
        .schema-card .sc-type { color: var(--muted); font-size: 11.5px; }
        .schema-card .sc-note { font-size: 11.5px; color: var(--muted); margin-top: 8px; padding-top: 6px; border-top: 1px solid var(--faint); line-height: 1.5; }

        /* ── REPRODUCIBILITY CARDS ── */
        .repro-grid { display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr; gap: 12px; margin: 16px 0 24px; }
        @media(max-width:640px) { .repro-grid { grid-template-columns: 1fr; } }
        .repro-card { background: var(--surface); border: 1px solid var(--rule); border-radius: 8px; padding: 14px 16px; }
        .repro-card .rc-icon  { font-size: 22px; margin-bottom: 7px; display: block; }
        .repro-card .rc-title { font-weight: 600; font-size: 13.5px; color: var(--ink); margin-bottom: 5px; }
        .repro-card .rc-body  { font-size: 12.5px; color: var(--muted); line-height: 1.55; }
        
        /* ── SECTION 2: CRITICAL ANALYSIS (cards) ── */
        .ca-hero {
        background: linear-gradient(135deg, #eef2f9 0%, #ffffff 55%, #eef2f9 100%);
        border: 1px solid var(--rule);
        border-radius: 12px;
        padding: 18px 20px;
        margin: 14px 0 22px;
        }
        .ca-hero strong { color: var(--accent); }
        .ca-hero p { margin: 8px 0 0; color: var(--ink2); font-size: 13.5px; line-height: 1.7; }
        
        .ca-two-col { display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr; gap: 14px; margin: 18px 0 8px; }
        @media(max-width: 860px){ .ca-two-col { grid-template-columns: 1fr; } }
        
        .ca-panel {
          background: var(--surface);
          border: 1px solid var(--rule);
          border-radius: 12px;
          overflow: hidden;
        }
        .ca-panel-head {
          padding: 14px 16px 12px;
          border-bottom: 1px solid var(--faint);
          display: flex;
          justify-content: space-between;
          align-items: baseline;
          gap: 12px;
        }
        .ca-panel-title {
          font-family: 'Lora', serif;
          font-weight: 700;
          font-size: 1.0rem;
          color: var(--ink);
          margin: 0;
        }
        .ca-panel-sub {
          font-family: 'IBM Plex Mono', monospace;
          font-size: 10px;
          font-weight: 700;
          letter-spacing: .12em;
          text-transform: uppercase;
          color: var(--muted);
          margin: 0;
          white-space: nowrap;
        }
        .ca-items { padding: 8px 0; }
        
        .ca-item {
          display: grid;
          grid-template-columns: 52px 1fr;
          gap: 14px;
          padding: 14px 16px;
          border-top: 1px solid var(--faint);
        }
        .ca-item:first-child { border-top: none; }
        
        .ca-num {
          width: 44px; height: 44px;
          border-radius: 12px;
          display: flex;
          align-items: center;
          justify-content: center;
          font-family: 'IBM Plex Mono', monospace;
          font-weight: 800;
          font-size: 13px;
        }
        .ca-num.s { background: #ecfdf5; border: 1px solid #a7f3d0; color: #047857; }
        .ca-num.w { background: #fffbeb; border: 1px solid #fde68a; color: #92400e; }
        
        .ca-body h4 {
          margin: 0 0 6px;
          font-family: 'Lora', serif;
          font-size: .98rem;
          font-weight: 700;
          line-height: 1.3;
        }
        .ca-body p {
          margin: 0;
          color: var(--ink2);
          font-size: 13.5px;
          line-height: 1.7;
        }
        </style>
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
library(knitr)
opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

---

# Team Information

| Name | Student ID |
|---|---|
| Adheesh | 2500670 |
| Shannon | 2403263 |
| Thiha Thein | 2502266 |
| Jing Hong | 2503049 |
| Zafri | 2403108 |

---

# 1. Introduction and Project Overview {#sec-original}

## 1.1 Our Project's Aim

Singapore's workforce has undergone a profound educational transformation over the past three decades. University-educated workers now make up the majority of employed residents in the 25–44 age bands, yet public debate continues to ask whether the labour market has truly absorbed this surge in graduate supply — or whether a structural mismatch has quietly emerged between qualifications and available jobs.

Our project seeks to answer three interrelated questions using official 2024 Ministry of Manpower data. 

**First**, how does educational attainment among *employed* Singaporeans actually vary across age groups — and does the composition differ meaningfully from what population-level data alone suggests?

**Second**, are there meaningful gender gaps in the education–employment relationship that aggregate statistics conceal? The original visualization hints at gender differences but its crowded layout makes them impossible to quantify.

**Third**, does employment rate data by age and sex support or challenge the narrative that younger, highly educated cohorts face disproportionate labour market difficulty?

Our improved chart aims answers all three questions in a single view by encoding four dimensions simultaneously: age group, sex, employment rate, and education composition. The redesign moves beyond the original chart's population-count framing to focus on the *employed* resident workforce, revealing where educational capital is concentrated, where gender gaps are widest, and which cohorts — if any — show signs of underemployment relative to their qualifications.

## 1.2 Source & Context

The original visualization was published by **FYT Consultants** in a blog post titled *"A Data-Driven Look at Singapore's Graduate Unemployment Debate"* (September 2025), available at:

> <https://www.fytconsultants.com/single-post/a-data-driven-look-at-singapore-s-graduate-unemployment-debate>

The underlying data comes from the **Singapore Department of Statistics (SingStat)**, specifically the annual dataset: *Singapore Residents Aged 25 Years and Over by Highest Qualification Attained (HQA), Sex and Age Group*.

## 1.2 Our Chosen Visualization

```{=html}
<div style="text-align:center; margin: 24px 0;">
  <img
    src="https://static.wixstatic.com/media/420198_c8e3f9f7fe3141eca81e18827ad2472d~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_705,h_1004,al_c,q_90,enc_avif,quality_auto/420198_c8e3f9f7fe3141eca81e18827ad2472d~mv2.png"
    alt="Original Visualization: Population by Education and Gender Over Time – 2024"
    style="max-width: 480px; width: 100%; border: 1px solid #dee2e6; border-radius: 8px; box-shadow: 0 4px 16px rgba(0,0,0,0.12);"
  />
  <p style="color: #666; font-size: 0.88rem; margin-top: 10px;">
    <em>Figure 1: Original Tableau Visualization — "Population by Education and Gender Over Time – 2024"</em><br>
    Source: FYT Consultants / Singapore Department of Statistics
  </p>
</div>
```


### 1.2.1 Visualization Structure and Design

The visualization selected is a **dual-panel stacked bar chart** titled *"Population by Education and Gender Over Time – 2024"*. The bar chart illustrates Singapore's resident population aged 25 and above, broken down simultaneously across three dimensions:

1. **Age group** — From ages 25–29 years old through to 65 years old & over
2. **Gender** — Females and Males displayed as adjacent bar pairs within each age group
3. **Highest Educational Attainment** — University, Diploma & Professional Qualification, Post Secondary (Non-Tertiary), Secondary, and Below Secondary

The chart comprises two vertically stacked panels:

- **Top panel (proportional / 100% stacked bars):** Shows the *percentage share* of each education level within a given age–gender group, enabling comparison of educational composition across cohorts.
- **Bottom panel (absolute count bars):** Shows the *actual number of residents* in each age–gender group, providing population size context that the proportional view alone cannot convey.

### 1.2.2 The Story It Tells

The visualization narrates a **generational shift in educational attainment in Singapore**. Several narratives emerge when analysing the stacked bar chart:

**Younger cohorts are markedly more educated.** In the 25–34 age bands, University (dark navy) dominates the top proportional panel, exceeding 50% for both males and females. This proportion of university graduates decreases dramatically as the age group increases.

**Older cohorts show high "Below Secondary" and "Secondary" attainment.** The Below Secondary (yellow) education level becomes prominent in the 60–64 and 65+ groups, reflecting limited access to higher levels of education in the earlier decades.

**The 65+ age group is by far the largest in absolute size.** The bottom panel reveals a striking spike in the 65+ bars compared to all other age groups, reflecting Singapore's ageing population and contextualising the large "Below Secondary" proportion in that cohort.

**Gender differences in educational attainment are visible but subtle.** In younger cohorts, females and males show comparable university rates; in older cohorts, males sometimes show slightly higher university proportions, reflecting historical gender gaps in access to education.

The bar chart's dual-panel design is the most distinctive structural feature — it attempts to showcase two stories at once: the composition story (top chart) and the population size story (bottom chart).

---

# 2.  Critical Analysis of Original Visualization {#sec-analysis}

```{=html}
<div class="ca-hero">
  <strong>What this section does:</strong>
  <p>We evaluate the original chart’s design choices by identifying what it does well (Strengths) and what limits its analytical value (Weaknesses). These points directly motivate the redesign decisions in Section 3.</p>
</div>
```

```{=html}
<div class="ca-two-col">

  <!-- Strengths -->
  <div class="ca-panel">
    <div class="ca-panel-head">
      <h3 class="ca-panel-title">Strengths</h3>
      <p class="ca-panel-sub">What works well</p>
    </div>

    <div class="ca-items">
      <div class="ca-item">
        <div class="ca-num s">S1</div>
        <div class="ca-body">
          <h4>Dual-panel design provides complementary context</h4>
          <p>The most significant design strength is the pairing of a 100% stacked bar (proportional) with an absolute count bar beneath it. A proportional chart alone can be misleading — a cohort with 80% university graduates might represent only a tiny absolute population. Thus, the bottom panel mitigates misinterpretation by revealing the raw counts, making the 65+ spike immediately apparent. This design decision showcases awareness of a well-known pitfall in compositional visualization.</p>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div class="ca-item">
        <div class="ca-num s">S2</div>
        <div class="ca-body">
          <h4>Consistent colour encoding across education levels</h4>
          <p>The 5 education levels are mapped to a sequential–diverging palette, with a legend attached for easy referencing. The dark navy (University) and medium blue (Diploma) occupy the "cool" end of the spectrum, while the yellow-green (Below Secondary) anchors the "warm" end. The colour logic is intuitive — darker/cooler tones are associated with higher attainment — making readability efficient.</p>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div class="ca-item">
        <div class="ca-num s">S3</div>
        <div class="ca-body">
          <h4>Multi-variable analysis in a single chart</h4>
          <p>The chart successfully visualizes the intersectional characteristics of age, gender, and education in a single view from a genuinely complex multidimensional dataset. Alternative approaches such as faceted line charts or heat maps would require the viewer to toggle between multiple panels to perceive these relationships together.</p>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div class="ca-item">
        <div class="ca-num s">S4</div>
        <div class="ca-body">
          <h4>Clear legend and descriptive subtitle</h4>
          <p>The legend is well-labelled, easy to understand, and directly matches the colour categories used in the bars. The subtitle explains what is being shown in both panels, which is necessary given the chart's complexity.</p>
        </div>
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>

  <!-- Weaknesses -->
  <div class="ca-panel">
    <div class="ca-panel-head">
      <h3 class="ca-panel-title">Weaknesses</h3>
      <p class="ca-panel-sub">What limits analysis</p>
    </div>

    <div class="ca-items">
      <div class="ca-item">
        <div class="ca-num w">W1</div>
        <div class="ca-body">
          <h4>Poor readability from severe x-axis label crowding</h4>
          <p>The age group labels are printed vertically above the top panel, while the gender labels ("Female" and "Male") are rotated vertically below the bottom panel. With 9 age groups × 2 genders = 18 bar pairs, the labels overlap and are difficult to read at normal resolution. The viewer must invest significant effort tracking which bar belongs to which age group.</p>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div class="ca-item">
        <div class="ca-num w">W2</div>
        <div class="ca-body">
          <h4>Visual discontinuity between the two panels</h4>
          <p>The top and bottom panels are not visually connected by gridlines, reference lines, or shading. The viewer must mentally align bars across the two panels to link a proportional observation with its absolute count. The lack of a linking visual device weakens the narrative connection that is the chart's core strength.</p>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div class="ca-item">
        <div class="ca-num w">W3</div>
        <div class="ca-body">
          <h4>Stacked bars make mid-segment comparison almost impossible</h4>
          <p>Since "Below Secondary" is the only category anchored to a common baseline (0%), all other education levels float at varying heights. Comparing the "Post Secondary (Non-Tertiary)" share between, say, 35–39 year-old females and 55–59 year-old males requires estimating floating segment boundaries across two different bars — a task that is cognitively very demanding.</p>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div class="ca-item">
        <div class="ca-num w">W4</div>
        <div class="ca-body">
          <h4>The 65+ absolute bar dwarfs all other bars, distorting the bottom panel</h4>
          <p>The 65+ cohort is so much larger (400K+) that all other bars in the bottom panel appear compressed. The y-axis scale accommodates the maximum value, reducing comparative resolution across all other age groups.</p>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div class="ca-item">
        <div class="ca-num w">W5</div>
        <div class="ca-body">
          <h4>No direct value annotations</h4>
          <p>Neither panel includes data labels or tooltips, as the visualization is a static export. The viewer cannot determine precise percentages or counts without a reference grid.</p>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div class="ca-item">
        <div class="ca-num w">W6</div>
        <div class="ca-body">
          <h4>Colour palette has accessibility concerns</h4>
          <p>The distinction between the light blue (Diploma & Professional Qualification) and light green (Post Secondary Non-Tertiary) segments is subtle and may be difficult for viewers with colour vision deficiencies.</p>
        </div>
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>

</div>
```

---

# 3. Proposed Improvements {#sec-improvements}

## 3.1 Enhancement Strategy

Going back to the six weaknesses we flagged in Section 2, we came up with six improvements that each target a specific problem in the original chart. We tried to be deliberate about this — rather than doing a general visual refresh, each change has a clear reason behind it. The table below shows exactly which weakness each improvement responds to, and we've also noted which of the original chart's genuine strengths we made sure to carry over.

```{=html}
<div class="imp-table-wrap">
<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>#</th>
      <th>Weakness Addressed</th>
      <th>Improvement</th>
      <th>Method / Technique</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>I1</td>
      <td><span class="badge badge-w">W1</span></td>
      <td>The original's 18 rotated labels make it genuinely hard to match a bar to its age group. We swap them out for a fixed centre column that sits between the male and female wings — every age label is now horizontal and tied directly to its row, so there's no guesswork involved.</td>
      <td>Flexbox butterfly layout; fixed-width centre column</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>I2</td>
      <td><span class="badge badge-w">W2</span> <span class="badge badge-w">W3</span></td>
      <td>Rather than two separate panels the reader has to mentally stitch together, we combine both into a single bar — the width shows employment rate and the coloured fill shows education composition. This eliminates the panel gap (W2) and also gives every segment a shared central spine, so you can actually compare the same education category across different age groups without floating baselines (W3).</td>
      <td>Dual-encoding on a single bar axis</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>I3</td>
      <td><span class="badge badge-w">W3</span></td>
      <td>We added a toggle that lets you click on any education category to hide it. When you do, the remaining categories automatically recalculate so they still add up to 100% — that way the proportions stay meaningful and you can cleanly compare any subset of education levels without the floating baseline problem.</td>
      <td>Interactive segment filter; live proportion recalculation</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>I4</td>
      <td><span class="badge badge-w">W4</span></td>
      <td>We use employment rate as bar <em>width</em> instead of raw population count. Since older cohorts have lower participation rates, their bars are naturally shorter — no single group blows up the scale, and all 12 age groups sit on the same 0–100% axis without the 65+ spike crushing everything else.</td>
      <td>Rate-normalised bar width instead of absolute count axis</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>I5</td>
      <td><span class="badge badge-w">W5</span></td>
      <td>The employment rate for each bar is shown as a small pill label right at the bar end, so the key number is always visible without needing to interact. Hovering brings up a full tooltip with the exact rate plus the education breakdown to 2 decimal places — recalculated on the fly based on whichever segments are currently active.</td>
      <td>Inline bar-end pill labels; floating rich tooltip on hover</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>I6</td>
      <td><span class="badge badge-w">W6</span></td>
      <td>The light blue and light green in the original are easy to mix up, particularly for anyone with colour vision deficiency. We switched to the Wong (2011) palette — dark gray, blue, orange, purple, and green — which keeps all five categories clearly distinguishable. The segment toggle also means colour is never the only cue.</td>
      <td>Wong (2011) CB-safe palette; interactive segment isolation</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>
</div>

<div class="strengths-preserved">
  <div class="sp-title">✅ Original Strengths Preserved</div>
  <div class="sp-row">
    <span class="sp-badge">S1</span>
    <span class="sp-text"><strong>Gender comparison is kept front and centre.</strong> The butterfly layout keeps males and females facing each other across a shared age spine, so the side-by-side gender comparison that made the original useful is still immediately visible — and actually easier to read now that the label clutter is gone.</span>
  </div>
  <div class="sp-row">
    <span class="sp-badge">S2</span>
    <span class="sp-text"><strong>One colour per education level, consistently applied.</strong> We kept the same logic of assigning a single colour to each education category throughout. The Wong palette is a swap made for accessibility reasons — the principle of consistent colour coding is preserved, and someone familiar with the original can still orient themselves quickly.</span>
  </div>
  <div class="sp-row">
    <span class="sp-badge">S3</span>
    <span class="sp-text"><strong>Still a single chart, not a dashboard of panels.</strong> Age group, gender, employment rate, and education breakdown are all readable from one view without needing to open a second panel or cross-reference another figure. We actually added a dimension compared to the original while keeping the single-view approach intact.</span>
  </div>
</div>
```

## 3.2 Justification for Redesign Approach

We decided early on not to rebuild the original Tableau chart directly. Part of this was practical — several of the problems we identified in Section 2 are genuinely unsolvable within a static format, so patching the layout would only get us so far. Rebuilding as a fully interactive HTML visualization gave us the room to address those issues properly. Three main reasons drove this decision:

```{=html}
<div class="justify-grid">
  <div class="jcard">
    <span class="jcard-icon">🔄</span>
    <div class="jcard-title">Interactivity Solves What Static Cannot</div>
    <div class="jcard-body">The original is a <strong>static image</strong>. The problems with floating segments (W3) and missing value labels (W5) are not design oversights — they are things a static chart simply cannot fix. Making it interactive is the actual solution: segment toggles give every category a consistent baseline, and hover tooltips let you read off exact figures on demand.</div>
  </div>
  <div class="jcard">
    <span class="jcard-icon">📐</span>
    <div class="jcard-title">Employment Rate Adds the Missing Half</div>
    <div class="jcard-body">The original only shows education composition — it does not tell you how many of those people are actually employed. By pulling in <strong>employment rates from MOM Table B1</strong>, we add that labour-market context directly into the same bar. Bar width shows participation, fill shows composition, and both questions get answered at once without needing a second panel.</div>
  </div>
  <div class="jcard">
    <span class="jcard-icon">💡</span>
    <div class="jcard-title">The Chart Explains Itself</div>
    <div class="jcard-body">Between the pill labels, the hover tooltip, and the interactive controls, most of what you'd normally need a caption to explain is just visible in the chart itself. A reader can isolate a cohort, toggle education levels, and read off exact figures <strong>without leaving the visualization</strong> — which we felt fulfilled the brief of both being analytically useful and looking professional.</div>
  </div>
</div>

<div class="callout-custom insight">
  <strong>🔍 Why a Butterfly Layout?</strong>
  Gender comparison is genuinely central to what this dataset is about, and the butterfly format puts that front and centre — males on the left, females on the right, age labels in the middle. You can scan any row horizontally and immediately see the gap between the two sides. As a bonus, it also takes care of the label problem naturally, since the central spine replaces all 18 rotated labels with one clean column.
</div>
```

## 3.3 Proposed Visualization Design

### 3.3.1 Interactive Butterfly Chart — Employment Rate by Age, Gender & Education Level (2024)

The chart below is our redesign. It shows **four dimensions at once** — age group, gender, employment rate, and education composition — all within a single interactive view. Bar width represents employment rate, and the coloured segments within each bar show how that group is split across the five education categories. Hover over any bar for exact figures, and use the controls at the top to filter by education category or age group.

```{=html}
<div class="dim-grid">
  <div class="dim-pill"><span class="dim-icon">👥</span><span class="dim-label">Age Group <span style="font-weight:300;font-size:12px">(y-axis, 12 groups)</span></span></div>
  <div class="dim-pill"><span class="dim-icon">⚧</span><span class="dim-label">Gender <span style="font-weight:300;font-size:12px">(left / right wings)</span></span></div>
  <div class="dim-pill"><span class="dim-icon">📊</span><span class="dim-label">Employment Rate % <span style="font-weight:300;font-size:12px">(bar width)</span></span></div>
  <div class="dim-pill"><span class="dim-icon">🎓</span><span class="dim-label">Education Share % <span style="font-weight:300;font-size:12px">(stacked fill colour)</span></span></div>
</div>

<!-- ═══ INTERACTIVE BUTTERFLY CHART ═══ -->
<div class="viz-wrap">
  <div class="controls-bar">
    <span class="ctrl-label">Show segments:</span>
    <div class="seg-toggles" id="segToggles"></div>
    <div class="highlight-select">
      <span class="ctrl-label">Filter ages:</span>
      <button id="ageFilterBtn" class="age-filter-btn">All ages ▾</button>
      <div id="ageDropdown" class="age-dropdown" style="display:none;">
        <div class="age-dd-header">
          <span style="font-size:11px;font-weight:600;color:var(--muted);text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:.06em">Select age groups</span>
          <div style="display:flex;gap:6px;">
            <button class="age-dd-action" id="selectAll">All</button>
            <button class="age-dd-action" id="clearAll">Clear</button>
          </div>
        </div>
        <div class="age-checkbox-grid" id="ageCheckboxes"></div>
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>

  <div class="gender-strip">
    <div class="gs-male">Male</div>
    <div class="gs-age">Age</div>
    <div class="gs-fem">Female</div>
  </div>

  <div class="row-group" id="rowGroup"></div>
  <div class="axis-strip" id="axisTicks"></div>
  <div class="x-label">Employment Rate (%)</div>
  <div class="legend" id="legend"></div>
  <p class="source">Source: MOM Comprehensive Labour Force Survey 2024 · Table B1 (employment rates by age and sex) · Table G4 (education counts by age and sex) · Manpower Research &amp; Statistics Department, Ministry of Manpower, Singapore.</p>
</div>

<div class="butterfly-tooltip" id="butterfly-tooltip"></div>

<script>
(function() {
  const EDU_LABELS = ["Below Secondary","Secondary","Post-Secondary (Non-Tertiary)","Diploma & Professional Qual.","Degree"];
  const EDU_COLORS = ["#5a5a5a","#0072b2","#e69f00","#7b2d8b","#009e73"];
  const AGE_GROUPS = ["15\u201319","20\u201324","25\u201329","30\u201334","35\u201339","40\u201344","45\u201349","50\u201354","55\u201359","60\u201364","65\u201369","70 & Over"];

  const EMP = {
    Male:  {"15\u201319":15.2,"20\u201324":52.0,"25\u201329":81.8,"30\u201334":93.3,"35\u201339":95.2,"40\u201344":94.9,"45\u201349":93.6,"50\u201354":89.7,"55\u201359":84.6,"60\u201364":76.0,"65\u201369":58.8,"70 & Over":28.8},
    Female:{"15\u201319":8.5, "20\u201324":47.9,"25\u201329":89.2,"30\u201334":89.6,"35\u201339":86.0,"40\u201344":84.1,"45\u201349":79.7,"50\u201354":77.2,"55\u201359":66.0,"60\u201364":56.3,"65\u201369":40.1,"70 & Over":17.1}
  };

  const EDU_RAW = {
    Male:  {"15\u201319":[1.1,5.8,8.7,0.8,0.0],"20\u201324":[0.9,6.0,19.8,29.9,2.5],"25\u201329":[1.4,6.2,11.0,29.1,51.5],"30\u201334":[2.3,9.5,12.0,30.8,77.7],"35\u201339":[3.4,9.1,12.5,29.5,85.4],"40\u201344":[4.7,10.9,12.7,30.4,84.3],"45\u201349":[7.9,14.2,9.6,29.6,73.4],"50\u201354":[11.7,19.9,14.0,27.1,62.7],"55\u201359":[24.2,22.9,15.2,19.1,39.2],"60\u201364":[33.8,27.1,17.4,13.4,22.8],"65\u201369":[28.7,18.9,12.5,8.5,8.8],"70 & Over":[30.3,12.9,11.0,5.5,6.3]},
    Female:{"15\u201319":[0.9,4.5,2.8,0.5,0.0],"20\u201324":[0.5,3.7,8.8,18.2,16.2],"25\u201329":[0.8,3.7,5.5,25.0,69.6],"30\u201334":[2.1,6.7,6.7,27.3,94.4],"35\u201339":[3.4,10.0,6.3,28.2,94.4],"40\u201344":[6.3,12.2,8.7,32.1,82.7],"45\u201349":[7.7,16.0,10.1,32.2,62.0],"50\u201354":[13.3,22.2,16.1,26.0,47.7],"55\u201359":[17.7,22.9,16.0,17.6,25.4],"60\u201364":[24.3,24.2,13.7,11.9,14.0],"65\u201369":[19.7,14.8,11.9,6.0,4.2],"70 & Over":[24.7,9.9,7.1,3.4,2.6]}
  };

  function normalizeSubset(raw, activeIndices) {
    const vals = activeIndices.map(i => raw[i]);
    const total = vals.reduce((a,b)=>a+b, 0);
    if (!total) return activeIndices.map(()=>0);
    const rounded = vals.map(v => Math.round(v/total*10000)/100);
    const diff = Math.round((100 - rounded.reduce((a,b)=>a+b,0))*100)/100;
    const maxI = rounded.indexOf(Math.max(...rounded));
    rounded[maxI] = Math.round((rounded[maxI]+diff)*100)/100;
    return rounded;
  }

  let activeSegs = new Set([0,1,2,3,4]);
  let selectedAges = new Set(AGE_GROUPS);
  const tooltip = document.getElementById('butterfly-tooltip');

  /* LEGEND */
  const legendEl = document.getElementById('legend');
  EDU_LABELS.forEach((l,i) => {
    const d = document.createElement('div'); d.className='leg-item';
    d.innerHTML=`<div class="leg-swatch" style="background:${EDU_COLORS[i]}"></div>${l}`;
    legendEl.appendChild(d);
  });

  /* SEG TOGGLES */
  const segTogglesCt = document.getElementById('segToggles');
  EDU_LABELS.forEach((l,i) => {
    const b = document.createElement('button');
    b.className='seg-btn active'; b.dataset.idx=i;
    b.innerHTML=`<span class="swatch" style="background:${EDU_COLORS[i]}"></span>${l.replace(' (Non-Tertiary)','').replace(' & Professional Qual.','+')}`;
    b.addEventListener('click', () => {
      if (activeSegs.has(i)) { if (activeSegs.size===1) return; activeSegs.delete(i); b.classList.remove('active'); }
      else { activeSegs.add(i); b.classList.add('active'); }
      rebuildAllBars();
    });
    segTogglesCt.appendChild(b);
  });

  /* AXIS */
  document.getElementById('axisTicks').innerHTML=`
  <div class="ax-half male"><div class="tick-list">${[100,80,60,40,20,0].map(t=>`<span>${t}%</span>`).join('')}</div></div>
  <div class="ax-age-gap"></div>
  <div class="ax-half fem"><div class="tick-list">${[0,20,40,60,80,100].map(t=>`<span>${t}%</span>`).join('')}</div></div>`;

  /* BUILD ROWS */
  const rowGroup = document.getElementById('rowGroup');
  const rowEls = {}, segDomEls = {};

  AGE_GROUPS.forEach(age => {
    const mE = EMP.Male[age], fE = EMP.Female[age];
    const row = document.createElement('div'); row.className='chart-row'; row.dataset.age=age;

    function makeWing(gender, emp) {
      const wing = document.createElement('div');
      wing.className = `wing ${gender==='Male'?'male':'fem'}`;
      const bw = document.createElement('div');
      bw.className='bar-wrap'; bw.style.width=`${emp}%`;
      const stk = document.createElement('div'); stk.className='stacked';
      const segs = EDU_LABELS.map((_, i) => {
        const s = document.createElement('div');
        s.className='seg'; s.dataset.segIdx=i;
        s.style.background = EDU_COLORS[i];
        s.style.width = '0%';
        stk.appendChild(s);
        return s;
      });
      bw.appendChild(stk);
      const tip = document.createElement('span');
      tip.className='emp-tip'; tip.textContent=`${emp}%`;
      bw.appendChild(tip);
      wing.appendChild(bw);
      return {wing, segs};
    }

    const m = makeWing('Male', mE);
    const ageCol = document.createElement('div');
    ageCol.className='age-col'; ageCol.textContent=age;
    const f = makeWing('Female', fE);

    row.appendChild(m.wing); row.appendChild(ageCol); row.appendChild(f.wing);
    rowGroup.appendChild(row);
    rowEls[age] = row;
    segDomEls[age] = {Male: m.segs, Female: f.segs};

    function buildTT(gender, emp) {
      const col = gender==='Male' ? '#1e3a5f' : '#7c2d12';
      const raw = EDU_RAW[gender][age];
      const activeArr = [...activeSegs].sort((a,b)=>a-b);
      const pcts = normalizeSubset(raw, activeArr);
      const rows = activeArr.map((si,j) => `
        <div class="tt-row">
          <span class="tt-lbl"><span class="tt-dot" style="background:${EDU_COLORS[si]}"></span>${EDU_LABELS[si]}</span>
          <span class="tt-val">${pcts[j].toFixed(2)}%</span>
        </div>`).join('');
      const hiddenCount = 5 - activeArr.length;
      const note = hiddenCount > 0 ? `<div class="tt-note">${hiddenCount} category${hiddenCount>1?'s':''} hidden — proportions recalculated for visible segments</div>` : '';
      return `<div class="tt-head" style="color:${col}">${age} · ${gender}</div>
<div class="tt-emp"><span style="color:var(--muted)">Employment Rate</span><span class="tt-val">${emp}%</span></div>
${rows}${note}`;
    }

    function mv(e){
      const x=e.clientX+16, y=e.clientY-8;
      const w=tooltip.offsetWidth, h=tooltip.offsetHeight;
      tooltip.style.left=(x+w>window.innerWidth-10?x-w-32:x)+'px';
      tooltip.style.top=(y+h>window.innerHeight-10?y-h:y)+'px';
    }
    m.wing.addEventListener('mouseenter', e=>{tooltip.style.display='block';tooltip.innerHTML=buildTT('Male',mE);mv(e);});
    m.wing.addEventListener('mousemove', mv);
    m.wing.addEventListener('mouseleave', ()=>tooltip.style.display='none');
    f.wing.addEventListener('mouseenter', e=>{tooltip.style.display='block';tooltip.innerHTML=buildTT('Female',fE);mv(e);});
    f.wing.addEventListener('mousemove', mv);
    f.wing.addEventListener('mouseleave', ()=>tooltip.style.display='none');
  });

  function rebuildAllBars() {
    const activeArr = [...activeSegs].sort((a,b)=>a-b);
    AGE_GROUPS.forEach(age => {
      ['Male','Female'].forEach(gender => {
        const raw = EDU_RAW[gender][age];
        const pcts = normalizeSubset(raw, activeArr);
        const segs = segDomEls[age][gender];
        segs.forEach(s => s.style.width = '0%');
        activeArr.forEach((si, j) => { segs[si].style.width = pcts[j] + '%'; });
      });
    });
  }

  rebuildAllBars();

  /* AGE FILTER */
  const ageBtn = document.getElementById('ageFilterBtn');
  const ageDd  = document.getElementById('ageDropdown');
  const ageCbs = document.getElementById('ageCheckboxes');

  AGE_GROUPS.forEach(age => {
    const lbl = document.createElement('label'); lbl.className='age-cb-item';
    const cb  = document.createElement('input'); cb.type='checkbox'; cb.checked=true; cb.value=age;
    cb.addEventListener('change', applyFilter);
    lbl.appendChild(cb); lbl.appendChild(document.createTextNode(age));
    ageCbs.appendChild(lbl);
  });
  ageBtn.addEventListener('click', e => {
    e.stopPropagation();
    const o = ageDd.style.display !== 'none';
    ageDd.style.display = o ? 'none' : 'block';
    ageBtn.classList.toggle('open', !o);
  });
  document.addEventListener('click', () => { ageDd.style.display='none'; ageBtn.classList.remove('open'); });
  ageDd.addEventListener('click', e => e.stopPropagation());
  document.getElementById('selectAll').addEventListener('click', () => { ageCbs.querySelectorAll('input').forEach(c=>c.checked=true); applyFilter(); });
  document.getElementById('clearAll').addEventListener('click', () => {
    const cs = [...ageCbs.querySelectorAll('input')];
    cs.forEach((c,i) => c.checked = i===0);
    applyFilter();
  });

  function applyFilter() {
    selectedAges.clear();
    ageCbs.querySelectorAll('input:checked').forEach(c => selectedAges.add(c.value));
    const n = selectedAges.size;
    ageBtn.textContent = n===AGE_GROUPS.length ? 'All ages ▾' : `${n} age${n>1?'s':''} selected ▾`;
    Object.entries(rowEls).forEach(([age, row]) => {
      row.classList.toggle('row-hidden', !selectedAges.has(age));
      row.classList.toggle('highlighted', selectedAges.has(age) && n < AGE_GROUPS.length);
    });
  }

  /* ENTRANCE ANIMATION */
  document.querySelectorAll('.bar-wrap').forEach((el,i) => {
    el.style.opacity='0'; el.style.transform='scaleX(0)';
    el.style.transformOrigin = el.closest('.male') ? 'right center' : 'left center';
    el.style.transition = `opacity .45s ${i*18}ms, transform .45s ${i*18}ms cubic-bezier(.22,.68,0,1.2)`;
    requestAnimationFrame(()=>requestAnimationFrame(()=>{ el.style.opacity='1'; el.style.transform='scaleX(1)'; }));
  });
  document.querySelectorAll('.emp-tip').forEach((el,i) => {
    el.style.opacity='0';
    setTimeout(()=>{ el.style.transition='opacity .3s'; el.style.opacity='1'; }, i*18+500);
  });
})();
</script>
```

### 3.3.2 Key Design Decisions

```{=html}
<ul class="decision-list">
  <li>
    <div class="decision-num">1</div>
    <div><strong>Diverging butterfly layout</strong> <span class="badge badge-i">I1 → W1</span><br>Placing males on the left and females on the right with age labels running down the centre was the most direct fix for the label problem. Each age group now has its own clearly defined row, and you can read every label without tilting your head. The left-right structure also makes gender comparison feel completely natural.</div>
  </li>
  <li>
    <div class="decision-num">2</div>
    <div><strong>Dual encoding: width × fill</strong> <span class="badge badge-i">I2 → W2 + W3</span><br>Making bar <em>width</em> represent employment rate and the <em>fill segments</em> represent education composition let us collapse the original's two disconnected panels into one bar. It also means all segments now share the same central spine, so you can compare the same education category across different rows without having to mentally account for shifting baselines.</div>
  </li>
  <li>
    <div class="decision-num">3</div>
    <div><strong>Live proportion recalculation on segment toggle</strong> <span class="badge badge-i">I3 → W3</span><br>When you hide an education category, we didn't just want to dim it — we wanted the remaining categories to stay proportionally honest. So when any segment is deselected, the others automatically recalculate to sum to 100%, keeping both the chart and the tooltip values consistent with whatever is currently shown.</div>
  </li>
  <li>
    <div class="decision-num">4</div>
    <div><strong>Rate-normalised bar width</strong> <span class="badge badge-i">I4 → W4</span><br>Switching from absolute population count to employment rate as the bar width means every age group sits on the same 0–100% scale. Older cohorts have lower participation rates so their bars are naturally shorter — there is no 65+ spike dominating the chart and making everything else look compressed.</div>
  </li>
  <li>
    <div class="decision-num">5</div>
    <div><strong>Prominent pill labels + rich tooltip</strong> <span class="badge badge-i">I5 → W5</span><br>The pill label at the end of each bar shows the employment rate at a glance, without needing to hover. The tooltip goes further — hover over any bar and you get the full education breakdown, recalculated for whichever segments are currently active, all adding to exactly 100%. These two layers together mean you can always get to a precise number, which simply was not possible with the original.</div>
  </li>
  <li>
    <div class="decision-num">6</div>
    <div><strong>Wong (2011) colourblind-safe palette</strong> <span class="badge badge-i">I6 → W6</span><br>The original's light blue and light green are genuinely easy to confuse, even without colour vision deficiency. The Wong (2011) palette — dark gray, blue, orange, purple, and green — was designed specifically to keep all five categories distinct under the most common forms of colour blindness. And since the segment toggle is always there, colour is never the only way to tell categories apart.</div>
  </li>
</ul>
```

### 3.3.3 Interaction Features

We built three interactive features into the chart, each one directly addressing something the static original could not do:

```{=html}
<div class="feature-grid">
  <div class="feature-card">
    <div class="fc-title"><span>🎛️</span> Segment Toggle</div>
    <p>Click any education category in the controls bar to remove it from the chart. The remaining segments automatically recalculate to sum to 100%, so you can compare any subset of education levels — say, just Degree vs Below Secondary — without floating baselines skewing what you see.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="feature-card">
    <div class="fc-title"><span>🔦</span> Multi-Select Age Filter</div>
    <p>The dropdown lets you pick any combination of age groups to focus on. Rows you deselect disappear from the chart entirely rather than just fading out — so if you only want to look at the prime working-age bands, the chart redraws around just those rows.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="feature-card">
    <div class="fc-title"><span>💬</span> Rich Tooltip on Hover</div>
    <p>Hovering over any bar shows the employment rate for that group alongside the full education breakdown — percentages to 2 decimal places, always adding to exactly 100%. If you've toggled some categories off, the tooltip only shows what's currently visible, so the numbers always match what you're looking at.</p>
  </div>
</div>
```

---


# 4. Data Sources & Data Quality {#sec-data}

## 4.1 Selected Datasets

Our improved butterfly visualization requires two datasets from the **Ministry of Manpower (MOM) Comprehensive Labour Force Survey 2024**, both sourced from the [MOM Statistics portal](https://stats.mom.gov.sg/Pages/Labour-Force-In-Singapore-2024.aspx). Both are contained within the official published workbooks and are used exclusively for the year **2024**.

```{=html}
<div class="ds-grid">

  <div class="ds-card">
    <div class="ds-badge">Primary Dataset</div>
    <div class="ds-title">Table B1 — Resident Employment Rate by Age and Sex, 2014–2024</div>
    <div class="ds-meta">
      <strong>File:</strong> LFR2024_SectionB_EmploymentRate.xlsx, Sheet <code>B1</code><br>
      <strong>Publisher:</strong> Manpower Research &amp; Statistics Dept, MOM Singapore<br>
      <strong>Unit:</strong> Per cent (%)<br>
      <strong>Coverage:</strong> 12 age groups × 3 sex categories (Total, Male, Female) × 11 years<br>
      <strong>We use:</strong> Male and Female rows, year 2024 column only
      <div style="margin-top:8px;">
        <span class="ds-tag">employment rate</span>
        <span class="ds-tag">age group</span>
        <span class="ds-tag">sex</span>
        <span class="ds-tag">2024</span>
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>

  <div class="ds-card">
    <div class="ds-badge">Secondary Dataset</div>
    <div class="ds-title">Table G4 — Employed Residents by Highest Qualification, Age and Sex, 2024</div>
    <div class="ds-meta">
      <strong>File:</strong> LFR2024_SectionG_MedianAgeDemographicProfile.xlsx, Sheet <code>G4</code><br>
      <strong>Publisher:</strong> Manpower Research &amp; Statistics Dept, MOM Singapore<br>
      <strong>Unit:</strong> Thousands of employed residents<br>
      <strong>Coverage:</strong> 12 age groups × 3 sex categories × 5 education levels<br>
      <strong>We use:</strong> Male and Female rows, all 5 education level columns, 2024 only
      <div style="margin-top:8px;">
        <span class="ds-tag">education level</span>
        <span class="ds-tag">age group</span>
        <span class="ds-tag">sex</span>
        <span class="ds-tag">count (thousands)</span>
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>

</div>
```

## 4.2 Justification for Dataset Selection

These two datasets were chosen over all other available MOM tables for three specific reasons that directly serve the improved visualization design:

**B1 resolves the 65+ distortion problem (W4).** The original visualization used absolute resident population counts as bar lengths, which caused the 65+ cohort to dwarf all other groups. By using employment *rates* from B1 as bar width instead, all 12 age groups are placed on a uniform 0–100% scale. The 65+ and 70+ cohorts naturally produce shorter bars that reflect their lower labour market participation, without any axis distortion.

**G4 provides the education composition for employed residents specifically.** The original chart used *all residents* (employed and non-employed) from SingStat population data. Our redesign focuses the education breakdown on *employed residents only* — the population actually participating in the labour market. This is a more analytically meaningful lens and directly justifies why B1 and G4 must come from the same MOM Labour Force Survey rather than from separate sources.

**Together they enable dual encoding across four dimensions.** No single table alone supports the butterfly chart's design. Joining B1 (employment rates) with G4 (education composition counts) creates the combined structure needed to encode age, sex, employment rate, and education simultaneously in one visualization.

```{=html}
<div class="callout-custom green">
  <strong>Minimum Dimension Requirement</strong>
  The project requires at least 4 dimensions in the final visualization. The joined dataset encodes <strong>4 dimensions simultaneously</strong>: Age Group (y-axis), Sex (left/right butterfly wings), Employment Rate % (bar width), and Education Level (stacked fill colour). This satisfies the requirement within a single chart without a secondary panel.
</div>
```

---

## 4.3 Raw Data Structure and Known Challenges

### 4.3.1 B1 Raw Layout

The B1 sheet does not follow a simple rectangular table structure. It uses a **multi-block layout** where each sex group (Total, Male, Female) occupies a separate vertical block of rows, preceded by several metadata rows. The structure is as follows:

```
Row 1       : "Contents"           ← sheet navigation label, skip
Row 2       : "TABLE B1"           ← title, skip
Row 3       : long title string    ← skip
Rows 4–6    : blank / units row    ← skip
Row 7       : [NULL, NULL, 2014, 2015, ..., 2024]   ← year headers
Row 8       : "Total", "Total", 64.5, ...            ← Total block header
Rows 9–19   : NULL, "15 - 19" ... "70 & Over"       ← 12 age rows (Total)
Rows 20–25  : blank + sub-aggregates                 ← skip
Row 27      : "Male", "Total", 73.3, ...             ← Male block header
Rows 28–38  : NULL, "15 - 19" ... "70 & Over"       ← 12 age rows (Male)
Rows 39–45  : blank + sub-aggregates                 ← skip
Row 46      : "Female", "Total", 56.2, ...           ← Female block header
Rows 47–57  : NULL, "15 - 19" ... "70 & Over"       ← 12 age rows (Female)
```

The year 2024 occupies **column 13** (columns 1–2 are labels; column 3 is 2014, incrementing one column per year).

### 4.3.2 G4 Raw Layout

G4 follows the same multi-block convention but has an additional complexity: the **education levels appear as column headers** (not rows), making it a two-dimensional table within each sex block.

```
Row 1       : "Contents"           ← skip
Row 2       : "TABLE G4"           ← skip
Row 3       : long title string    ← skip  
Rows 4–6    : blank / units row    ← skip
Row 7       : ["", NULL, "Total", "Below Secondary", "Secondary",
               "Post-Secondary (Non-Tertiary)",
               "Diploma & Professional Qualification", "Degree"]
Row 8       : "Total", "Total", 2365.6, 271.7, ...   ← Both sexes total block
Rows 9–19   : NULL, "15 - 19" ... "70 & Over"        ← 12 age rows (Both)
Row 20      : blank separator
Row 21      : "Male", "Total", 1238.0, 150.2, ...    ← Male block header
Rows 22–32  : NULL, "15 - 19" ... "70 & Over"        ← 12 age rows (Male)
Row 33      : blank separator
Row 34      : "Female", "Total", 1127.6, 121.5, ...  ← Female block header
Rows 35–45  : NULL, "15 - 19" ... "70 & Over"        ← 12 age rows (Female)
```

### 4.3.3 Identified Data Quality Challenges

The table below catalogues every known structural issue in the raw files and assigns a severity rating based on how likely it is to cause silent data errors if not handled correctly.

```{=html}
<table class="challenge-table">
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>ID</th>
      <th>File / Sheet</th>
      <th>Challenge</th>
      <th>Severity</th>
      <th>Planned Resolution</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>C1</td>
      <td>Both files</td>
      <td>Multiple metadata header rows precede the data table. Reading the file naively (e.g. with default <code>read_excel()</code>) will consume header rows as data rows.</td>
      <td><span class="sev-high">High</span></td>
      <td>Use <code>col_names = FALSE</code> on ingestion to preserve all rows, then extract data using fixed row offsets derived from visual inspection and documented as named constants in the code.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>C2</td>
      <td>Both files</td>
      <td>Data is split across three vertical blocks (Total / Male / Female) within a single sheet. There are no column markers indicating which block a row belongs to — only positional structure.</td>
      <td><span class="sev-high">High</span></td>
      <td>Extract each gender block independently using its known start row. Male and Female start rows are stored as named constants (<code>B1_MALE_START</code>, <code>G4_FEMALE_START</code>, etc.) so the logic is transparent and auditable.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>C3</td>
      <td>G4</td>
      <td>Negligible or suppressed counts are represented as the string <code>"-"</code> rather than <code>0</code> or <code>NA</code>. This occurs for cells such as the 15–19 Degree group where the count is effectively zero. Calling <code>as.numeric()</code> on <code>"-"</code> produces <code>NA</code>, which would silently corrupt proportion calculations.</td>
      <td><span class="sev-high">High</span></td>
      <td>Explicitly test for <code>"-"</code> before numeric coercion and replace with <code>0</code>. This is the statistically correct interpretation — the count is not missing, it is zero or suppressed due to rounding.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>C4</td>
      <td>G4</td>
      <td>Counts are published as <strong>rounded thousands</strong> (e.g. 132.3 means 132,300 people). Because of this rounding, the five education-level counts within an age × sex group will not sum to exactly the published total. Proportion sums will therefore deviate slightly from 1.0.</td>
      <td><span class="sev-med">Medium</span></td>
      <td>Compute proportions as <code>count / total</code> using the published total as the denominator. Accept deviations within ±2% as a known rounding artefact. Validate and flag in code comments. Units (thousands) will be retained throughout — no multiplication to raw counts needed.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>C5</td>
      <td>B1</td>
      <td>The sheet contains sub-aggregate rows (e.g. "15–24", "25–64", "55–64") interleaved between the 12 individual age-group rows and the next sex block. These could be accidentally included in extraction if row offsets are miscounted.</td>
      <td><span class="sev-med">Medium</span></td>
      <td>Extract exactly 12 rows starting from the documented start row for each sex block. The 12 individual age groups immediately follow the block header row with no interruption. A spot-check assertion will confirm that the 12th extracted row corresponds to "70 & Over".</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>C6</td>
      <td>Both files</td>
      <td>R's <code>read_excel()</code> assigns generic column names (<code>...1</code>, <code>...2</code>, etc.) when <code>col_names = FALSE</code> is used. Columns must be referenced by numeric index rather than name, which can be brittle if the sheet layout shifts in a future MOM release.</td>
      <td><span class="sev-low">Low</span></td>
      <td>Reference columns by numeric index constants only (<code>B1_COL_2024 &lt;- 13L</code>, <code>G4_COL_TOTAL &lt;- 3L</code>, etc.). All index constants are defined together in a single named-constants block at the start of the pipeline and are independently verifiable against the raw sheet.</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>
```

---

# 5. Data Engineering Workflow  {#sec-workflow}

## 5.1 Planned Data Engineering Pipeline 

The pipeline proceeds through five sequential stages. Each stage has a clearly defined input, a set of operations, and a defined output that feeds the next stage.

```{=html}
<div class="callout-custom">
  <strong>Pipeline Principle</strong>
  Each stage is implemented as a named R function or a clearly commented code block. No stage modifies a previous stage's output in-place — transformations always produce new objects. This ensures every intermediate state is inspectable and the pipeline can be re-run cleanly from any stage.
</div>

<div class="pipeline-wrap">

  <div class="pipe-step">
    <div class="pipe-num">1</div>
    <div class="pipe-body">
      <div class="pipe-title">Ingest Raw Excel Files</div>
      <div class="pipe-desc">
        Both <code>.xlsx</code> workbooks will be loaded using <code>readxl::read_excel()</code> with <code>col_names = FALSE</code> and no row skipping, so that all rows — including metadata headers — are preserved as data rows. This gives us full control over which rows are treated as headers versus data. The <code>readxl</code> package is used specifically because it does not require a Java runtime, unlike the <code>xlsx</code> package, making the pipeline more portable across team members' machines. Sheets are specified by name (<code>"B1"</code>, <code>"G4"</code>) rather than position to guard against sheet order changes in future MOM releases.
      </div>
      <div class="pipe-tags">
        <span class="pipe-tag">readxl::read_excel()</span>
        <span class="pipe-tag">col_names = FALSE</span>
        <span class="pipe-tag">sheet = "B1" / "G4"</span>
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>

  <div class="pipe-step">
    <div class="pipe-num">2</div>
    <div class="pipe-body">
      <div class="pipe-title">Extract 2024 Cross-Section by Gender Block</div>
      <div class="pipe-desc">
        Using the row offset constants documented in §5.2, each gender block (Male, Female) is extracted from both sheets independently by slicing exactly 12 consecutive rows — one per age group. For B1, column 13 (year 2024) is extracted from each block. For G4, the total column and the five education-level columns are extracted from each block. The result of this stage is four raw tibbles: <code>emp_male</code>, <code>emp_female</code>, <code>edu_male</code>, <code>edu_female</code>. A spot-check will confirm that the extracted Male 30–34 employment rate matches the published value of 93.3%.
      </div>
      <div class="pipe-tags">
        <span class="pipe-tag">row slicing [start:(start+11)]</span>
        <span class="pipe-tag">column indexing by constant</span>
        <span class="pipe-tag">as.numeric() coercion</span>
        <span class="pipe-tag">"-" → 0 for G4</span>
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>

  <div class="pipe-step">
    <div class="pipe-num">3</div>
    <div class="pipe-body">
      <div class="pipe-title">Reshape to Long (Tidy) Format and Standardise Labels</div>
      <div class="pipe-desc">
        The four extracted tibbles are combined using <code>dplyr::bind_rows()</code> — first <code>emp_male</code> and <code>emp_female</code> into <code>emp_2024</code> (24 rows), then <code>edu_male</code> and <code>edu_female</code> into <code>edu_2024</code> (120 rows). Column names are assigned explicitly as part of the extraction step: <code>sex</code>, <code>age</code>, <code>emp_rate</code> for the employment table; <code>sex</code>, <code>age</code>, <code>education</code>, <code>count_k</code>, <code>total_k</code> for the education table. Age group and education level columns are converted to ordered <code>factor</code> types with levels defined by constants, which guarantees consistent ordering throughout all downstream transformations and chart rendering.
      </div>
      <div class="pipe-tags">
        <span class="pipe-tag">dplyr::bind_rows()</span>
        <span class="pipe-tag">factor(..., levels = AGE_LABELS)</span>
        <span class="pipe-tag">factor(..., levels = EDU_LABELS)</span>
        <span class="pipe-tag">explicit column naming</span>
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>

  <div class="pipe-step">
    <div class="pipe-num">4</div>
    <div class="pipe-body">
      <div class="pipe-title">Derive Proportions, Bar Extents, and Butterfly Coordinates</div>
      <div class="pipe-desc">
        This is the core feature engineering stage. Three new columns are derived. First, <strong>education proportion</strong> (<code>prop</code>) is computed as <code>count_k / total_k</code> within each age × sex group using <code>dplyr::group_by()</code> + <code>mutate()</code>. Second, <strong>bar extent</strong> (<code>bar_extent</code>) is computed as <code>emp_rate × prop</code> — this is the dual-encoding that maps employment rate to bar length and education composition to fill colour simultaneously. Third, <strong>cumulative stacking coordinates</strong> (<code>xmin2</code>, <code>xmax2</code>) are computed using <code>cumsum()</code> within each group, then sign-flipped for the Male wing so that male bars extend leftward (negative x-axis) to create the butterfly layout.
      </div>
      <div class="pipe-tags">
        <span class="pipe-tag">prop = count_k / total_k</span>
        <span class="pipe-tag">bar_extent = emp_rate × prop</span>
        <span class="pipe-tag">cumsum() for stacking</span>
        <span class="pipe-tag">sign flip for male wing</span>
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>

  <div class="pipe-step">
    <div class="pipe-num">5</div>
    <div class="pipe-body">
      <div class="pipe-title">Join, Validate, and Export Visualization-Ready Objects</div>
      <div class="pipe-desc">
        The education long table and the employment rate table are joined on <code>sex</code> and <code>age</code> using <code>dplyr::left_join()</code>, with the education table as the left (primary) side. After joining, four automated validation checks will run: (1) a completeness check that the joined table has exactly 120 rows; (2) a zero-NA check across all visualization-critical columns; (3) a proportion-sum check that all age × sex groups sum within ±2% of 1.0; and (4) a plausibility check that prime working-age (35–54) employment rates exceed 75% for both sexes. Two final objects are exported for the visualization section: <code>employment_data</code> (120 rows, the main chart data) and <code>emp_pills</code> (24 rows, bar-end label data).
      </div>
      <div class="pipe-tags">
        <span class="pipe-tag">left_join(by = c("sex","age"))</span>
        <span class="pipe-tag">completeness assertion</span>
        <span class="pipe-tag">NA check</span>
        <span class="pipe-tag">proportion-sum check</span>
        <span class="pipe-tag">plausibility stopifnot()</span>
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>

</div>
```

---

## 5.2 Planned Cleaning and Transformation Decisions

Every cleaning choice is listed below with an explicit rationale. This ensures that no transformation is implicit or invisible to future readers of the code.

| # | Issue | Decision | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|
| D1 | Metadata header rows above data | Skip using fixed row offset constants; never use `skip =` argument in `read_excel()` | `skip =` silently discards rows; constants are self-documenting and verifiable against the raw sheet |
