| D2 | `"-"` string in G4 education counts | Replace with `0` before numeric coercion | Statistically correct — these are suppressed near-zero counts, not missing values; avoids silent `NA` propagation into proportion calculations |
| D3 | Counts published as rounded thousands | Retain in thousands; compute proportions from published totals | Multiplying to raw counts introduces false precision; thousands are sufficient for proportions and the chart does not show absolute counts |
| D4 | Wide format (education as columns) | Reshape to long within the extraction helper function | Long format is required by `ggplot2`'s `geom_rect()` for stacked bar rendering; one row per sex × age × education |
| D5 | No common baseline for education stacking | Pre-compute cumulative `xmin`/`xmax` positions before plotting | Passing raw proportions to `position_stack` in ggplot2 does not support the sign-flip required for the butterfly layout; pre-computation gives full control |
| D6 | Male bars must mirror to negative x-axis | Apply sign flip after cumulative sum: `xmin2 = -xmax`, `xmax2 = -xmin` | Separates the stacking arithmetic cleanly from the butterfly layout logic; the stacking step is sex-agnostic, the flip step handles the visual layout |
| D7 | Unordered factor levels would scramble stacking | Convert `age` and `education` to ordered factors at reshape stage | ggplot2 respects factor levels for ordering; declaring levels once at the reshape stage guarantees consistent order in all downstream objects |

---

## 5.3 Target Output Schema

Two objects will be passed from the data engineering pipeline to the visualization section. Their schemas are defined here so that the visualization code can be written against a known, stable interface.

```{=html}
<div class="schema-wrap">

  <div class="schema-card">
    <div class="sc-head">employment_data &nbsp;·&nbsp; 120 rows</div>
    <div class="sc-row"><span class="sc-col">age</span>       <span class="sc-type">factor (12 levels, ordered)</span></div>
    <div class="sc-row"><span class="sc-col">sex</span>       <span class="sc-type">factor (Male / Female)</span></div>
    <div class="sc-row"><span class="sc-col">education</span> <span class="sc-type">factor (5 levels, ordered)</span></div>
    <div class="sc-row"><span class="sc-col">emp_rate</span>  <span class="sc-type">numeric — employment rate %</span></div>
    <div class="sc-row"><span class="sc-col">prop</span>      <span class="sc-type">numeric — education share (0–1)</span></div>
    <div class="sc-row"><span class="sc-col">count_k</span>   <span class="sc-type">numeric — employed residents (000s)</span></div>
    <div class="sc-row"><span class="sc-col">total_k</span>   <span class="sc-type">numeric — total in age × sex group (000s)</span></div>
    <div class="sc-row"><span class="sc-col">bar_extent</span><span class="sc-type">numeric — emp_rate × prop</span></div>
    <div class="sc-row"><span class="sc-col">xmin2</span>     <span class="sc-type">numeric — segment left edge</span></div>
    <div class="sc-row"><span class="sc-col">xmax2</span>     <span class="sc-type">numeric — segment right edge</span></div>
    <div class="sc-note">Primary chart data. One row per age × sex × education combination. <code>xmin2</code> and <code>xmax2</code> are pre-computed for <code>geom_rect()</code>; negative values indicate male wing.</div>
  </div>

  <div class="schema-card">
    <div class="sc-head">emp_pills &nbsp;·&nbsp; 24 rows</div>
    <div class="sc-row"><span class="sc-col">age</span>      <span class="sc-type">factor (12 levels, ordered)</span></div>
    <div class="sc-row"><span class="sc-col">sex</span>      <span class="sc-type">factor (Male / Female)</span></div>
    <div class="sc-row"><span class="sc-col">emp_rate</span> <span class="sc-type">numeric — employment rate %</span></div>
    <div class="sc-row"><span class="sc-col">bar_end</span>  <span class="sc-type">numeric — signed bar-end x position</span></div>
    <div class="sc-note">Label data for employment rate pill annotations at each bar end. One row per age × sex group. <code>bar_end</code> is negative for males, positive for females.</div>
  </div>

</div>

<div class="callout-custom green">
  <strong>Four Dimensions Confirmed</strong>
  The <code>employment_data</code> schema encodes exactly four dimensions used by the butterfly chart: <strong>age</strong> (y-axis), <strong>sex</strong> (left/right wings via sign of xmin2/xmax2), <strong>emp_rate</strong> (total bar width), and <strong>education</strong> (stacked fill segments). A fifth dimension — <strong>count_k</strong> — is available for tooltip display. This satisfies and exceeds the project's 4-dimension minimum requirement.
</div>
```

---

## 5.4 Illustrative Code Structure

The final pipeline code will follow the structure below. This is not the complete working code — full annotated R code will be provided in the final submission — but it demonstrates the planned function signatures and data flow.

```r
# ── STAGE 1: Ingest ────────────────────────────────────────────
raw_b1 <- read_excel(path_b, sheet = "B1", col_names = FALSE)
raw_g4 <- read_excel(path_g, sheet = "G4", col_names = FALSE)

# ── STAGE 2: Extract 2024 cross-section ────────────────────────
# A named helper function per table type:
extract_emp_rates()    # args: raw sheet, start row, sex label, col index
extract_edu_counts()   # args: raw sheet, start row, sex label, col indices

emp_2024 <- bind_rows(
  extract_emp_rates(raw_b1, B1_MALE_START,   "Male",   B1_COL_2024),
  extract_emp_rates(raw_b1, B1_FEMALE_START, "Female", B1_COL_2024)
)

edu_2024 <- bind_rows(
  extract_edu_counts(raw_g4, G4_MALE_START,   "Male",   ...),
  extract_edu_counts(raw_g4, G4_FEMALE_START, "Female", ...)
)

# ── STAGE 3: Reshape and label ──────────────────────────────────
# Factor levels applied at this stage — once, and only here
edu_2024 <- edu_2024 |>
  mutate(
    age       = factor(age,       levels = AGE_LABELS),
    education = factor(education, levels = EDU_LABELS)
  )

# ── STAGE 4: Feature engineering ───────────────────────────────
edu_2024 <- edu_2024 |>
  group_by(sex, age) |>
  mutate(prop = count_k / total_k)   # education share within group

viz_long <- edu_2024 |>
  left_join(emp_2024, by = c("sex", "age")) |>
  mutate(bar_extent = emp_rate * prop) |>
  group_by(sex, age) |>
  mutate(
    xmax  = cumsum(bar_extent),
    xmin  = xmax - bar_extent,
    xmin2 = if_else(sex == "Male", -xmax, xmin),   # butterfly flip
    xmax2 = if_else(sex == "Male", -xmin, xmax)
  )

# ── STAGE 5: Validate and export ───────────────────────────────
stopifnot(nrow(viz_long) == 120)          # completeness
stopifnot(sum(is.na(viz_long)) == 0)      # no NAs
# ... proportion-sum and plausibility checks

employment_data    <- viz_long |> select(age, sex, education, emp_rate,
                                    prop, count_k, total_k, xmin2, xmax2)
emp_pills    <- ...   # 24-row summary for bar-end labels
```

```{=html}
<div class="callout-custom amber">
  <strong>Note on Code Status</strong>
  The pseudocode above illustrates the intended function signatures and data flow. The complete, fully annotated, and tested R implementation — with all validation outputs and intermediate data previews — will be provided in the final Week 13 submission.
</div>
```

---

## 5.5 Reproducibility Considerations

```{=html}
<div class="repro-grid">

  <div class="repro-card">
    <span class="rc-icon">📁</span>
    <div class="rc-title">File Structure</div>
    <div class="rc-body">Both <code>.xlsx</code> source files will be stored in a <code>datasets/</code> subfolder within the project root. File paths will be resolved programmatically relative to the <code>.qmd</code> document's own directory using <code>knitr::current_input()</code>, so no absolute paths are hard-coded. The pipeline will work on any team member's machine without path editing.</div>
  </div>

  <div class="repro-card">
    <span class="rc-icon">📦</span>
    <div class="rc-title">Package Management</div>
    <div class="rc-body">All required packages (<code>readxl</code>, <code>dplyr</code>, <code>tidyr</code>, <code>purrr</code>, <code>ggplot2</code>, <code>scales</code>) are loaded at the top of the pipeline section in a single <code>library()</code> block. Package versions will be captured and displayed using <code>sessionInfo()</code> at the end of the section, enabling any reviewer to verify the exact environment used for the final submission.</div>
  </div>

  <div class="repro-card">
    <span class="rc-icon">🔒</span>
    <div class="rc-title">Source File Integrity</div>
    <div class="rc-body">MD5 checksums of both source <code>.xlsx</code> files will be recorded using <code>tools::md5sum()</code> and printed within the document. This allows any reader to verify that the files used to produce the outputs are the original unmodified MOM published files, and provides a clear signal if files are ever accidentally replaced or updated.</div>
  </div>

  <div class="repro-card">
    <span class="rc-icon">📌</span>
    <div class="rc-title">Explicit Constants</div>
    <div class="rc-body">All row offsets and column indices derived from visual inspection of the raw sheets are stored as named constants (e.g. <code>B1_MALE_START &lt;- 28L</code>, <code>G4_COL_TOTAL &lt;- 3L</code>) in a single dedicated code block. No magic numbers appear inside extraction functions. If MOM updates the file layout, only the constants block needs updating — the extraction logic is unaffected.</div>
  </div>

  <div class="repro-card">
    <span class="rc-icon">✅</span>
    <div class="rc-title">Built-in Assertions</div>
    <div class="rc-body">Four automated validation checks (<code>stopifnot()</code> assertions) will be embedded directly in the pipeline: row count completeness, zero-NA check, proportion-sum tolerance, and prime-age plausibility. If any check fails, the document will halt with an informative error message rather than silently producing incorrect output. This makes data errors impossible to overlook during rendering.</div>
  </div>

  <div class="repro-card">
    <span class="rc-icon">🔄</span>
    <div class="rc-title">No External Dependencies</div>
    <div class="rc-body">The pipeline requires no internet connection, no database connections, and no API calls. All data is loaded from local files bundled with the project. The Quarto document can be rendered offline on any machine with R and the listed packages installed. There are no stochastic operations, so no random seed is needed — the output is fully deterministic.</div>
  </div>

</div>
```

---

# 6. Data Analysis Plan {#sec-data-analysis}

## 6.1 What This Analysis Is For

Our butterfly chart already shows the patterns visually, but this section adds a few simple calculations so we can state the key findings with clear numbers.

- **(A1)** Which age groups show the largest **male–female employment rate gap**
- **(A2)** How **Secondary school** qualification share differs across female age groups
- **(A3)** Whether age groups with higher **Degree** share also tend to have higher **employment rates**

```{=html}
<div class="feature-grid">
  <div class="feature-card">
    <div class="fc-title"><span>🧾</span> Basic & clear</div>
    <p>We will only use descriptive metrics (differences, shares, simple indices).</p>
  </div>
  <div class="feature-card">
    <div class="fc-title"><span>🔍</span> Supports the chart</div>
    <p>Each calculation corresponds to what we can see in our new butterfly chart.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="feature-card">
    <div class="fc-title"><span>📊</span> Produces outputs</div>
    <p>Each method produces either a table or a simple plot.</p>
  </div>
</div>
```

---

## 6.2 Measures We Will Compute

```{=html}
<div class="schema-wrap">

  <div class="schema-card">
    <div class="sc-head">Employment gap measures (B1, 2024)</div>
    <div class="sc-row"><span class="sc-col">Employment rate</span><span class="sc-type">emp_rate (%, by age × sex)</span></div>
    <div class="sc-row"><span class="sc-col">Gender employment gap</span><span class="sc-type">Gap = Male − Female (pp)</span></div>
    <div class="sc-note">This quantifies the left/right bar-width differences you see in the butterfly chart.</div>
  </div>

  <div class="schema-card">
    <div class="sc-head">Education composition measures (G4, 2024)</div>
    <div class="sc-row"><span class="sc-col">Education share</span><span class="sc-type">share = count_k / total_k</span></div>
    <div class="sc-row"><span class="sc-col">Secondary share focus</span><span class="sc-type">share_secondary (by age × sex)</span></div>
    <div class="sc-row"><span class="sc-col">Education concentration</span><span class="sc-type">HHI = Σ(share²)</span></div>
    <div class="sc-note">Share supports the stacked colours; HHI tells whether a cohort is dominated by one level or more mixed.</div>
  </div>

</div>
```

---

## 6.3 Planned Analyses 

### A1 — Gender Employment Gap by Age (where is the gap biggest?)

**Question:** In 2024, which age group show the largest difference in employment rate between males and females?

**Metric (percentage points):**
- **Gap(age) = emp_rate_male(age) − emp_rate_female(age)**

**Output:** A ranked table + a dot plot (or bar chart) of gaps across age bands.

```{=html}
<div class="callout-custom green">
  <strong>Insight:</strong>
  We expect the employment gaps to be small in early working ages but widen at later ages, indicating differing labour force participation patterns by sex.
</div>
```

---

### A2 — “Secondary School Qualification” Share Among Employed Females 

**Question:** In 2024, for employed females, how does the share of **Secondary school** qualification change across age groups?

**Metric:**
- **SecondaryShare_female(age) = SecondaryCount_female(age) ÷ TotalEmployed_female(age)**

**Output:** A single line/dot plot: age bands on y-axis, secondary share on x-axis (female only).  
(We can also add male as a comparison line.)

```{=html}
<div class="callout-custom green">
  <strong>Insight:</strong>
  In 2024, we expect Secondary school qualification share among employed females to be lower in younger age groups but to rise strongly in older age groups, because diploma/degree pathways were less accessible for older cohorts and Secondary was more common as a highest qualification in that period.
</div>
```

---

### A3 — Education vs Employment (do more degree-heavy cohorts also have higher employment rates?)

**Question:** Across 2024 age groups, is higher **Degree qualification share** associated with higher **employment rate**?

**Metrics:**
- **DegreeShare(age, sex) = DegreeCount ÷ TotalEmployed**
- Compare with **emp_rate(age, sex)**

**Output:** 
A scatter plot (per sex):  
x = Degree share, y = employment rate, points labelled by age group.

```{=html}
<div class="callout-custom green">
  <strong>Insight:</strong>
  In 2024, age groups with a higher Degree share generally also show higher employment rates, especially in the prime working ages (25–54). However, the relationship weakens after 55 because employment rates fall sharply due to labour-force exit/retirement, even if some Degree holders remain. This would suggest education composition aligns with employment participation, but age effects are the dominant driver at older ages.
</div>
```


---

## 6.4 Summary: What We Produce (2024)

```{=html}
<table class="summary-analysis-table">
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>ID</th>
      <th>Question Answered (2024)</th>
      <th>Output</th>
      <th>Supports Which Insight</th>
      <th>Data Needed</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>A1</td>
      <td>Where is the gender employment gap largest?</td>
      <td><span class="out-badge out-dot">Gap plot + table</span></td>
      <td>Bar-width differences (employment rate)</td>
      <td>B1 (2024 only)</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>A2</td>
      <td>How does female Secondary school qualification share differ by age group?</td>
      <td><span class="out-badge out-line">Line / dot plot</span></td>
      <td>Education composition shift (Secondary)</td>
      <td>G4 (2024 only)</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>A3</td>
      <td>Is degree share linked to employment rate across age groups?</td>
      <td><span class="out-badge out-dual">Scatter plot</span></td>
      <td>Dual-encoding logic (width × fill)</td>
      <td>B1 + G4 (2024 only)</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>
```

---

# 7. Planned Work Distribution {#sec-workdist}

| Team Member | Primary Tasks | Est. Hours |
|---|---|---|
| Adheesh | Proposed improvements with detailed justifications | ~12 hrs |
| Shannon | Data Engineering Workflow & Appendix | ~12 hrs |
| Thiha Thein Kevin | Data sources + Planned data analysis | ~10 hrs |
| Jing Hong | Edit urself | ~12 hrs |
| Zafri | Edit urself | ~12 hrs |
| All Members | Content Moderation & Document Formatting | ~8 hrs each |

---

# 8. References

## 8.1 Article Source

- D. Yuen, "A Data-Driven Look at Singapore's 'Graduate Unemployment' Debate," FYT Consultants, Sep. 17, 2025. <https://www.fytconsultants.com/single-post/a-data-driven-look-at-singapore-s-graduate-unemployment-debate>

## 8.2 Data Sources

- Singapore Department of Statistics, 2026, "Singapore Residents Aged 25 Years & Over By Highest Qualification Attained (HQA), Sex And Age Group, Annual," data.gov.sg. <https://data.gov.sg/datasets/d_1020b8ee03923042a8ab8e26eef58093/view>

- Ministry of Manpower, "Labour Force in Singapore 2024," Mom.gov.sg, 2024. <https://stats.mom.gov.sg/Pages/Labour-Force-In-Singapore-2024.aspx>

## 8.3 Design Reference

- B. Wong, “Points of View: Color Blindness,” Nature Methods, vol.8, no. 6, pp. 441–441, May 2011, doi: <https://doi.org/10.1038/nmeth.1618> 

---

# 9. Appendix: Data Dictionary {#sec-appendix}

The two tidy objects produced by the data engineering pipeline are documented
below. Every column used by the visualization or tooltip layer is defined here
with its source, unit, valid range, and role in the chart.

## A.1 `employment_data` — Primary Visualization Table

120 rows · one row per **age group × sex × education level** combination.

| Column | R Type | Source | Unit / Range | Role in Chart |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `age` | `factor` (12 levels) | B1 & G4 row labels | Age band e.g. `"25-29"` | Y-axis position |
| `sex` | `factor` (2 levels) | B1 & G4 block headers | `"Male"` or `"Female"` | Left / right butterfly wing |
| `education` | `factor` (5 levels) | G4 column headers | See level definitions below | Stacked fill colour |
| `emp_rate` | `numeric` | B1, Sheet B1, col 13 | Percent, 0–100 | Total bar width |
| `prop` | `numeric` | Derived: `count_k / total_k` | Proportion, 0–1 | Each segment's share of bar width |
| `count_k` | `numeric` | G4, education columns | Thousands of employed residents | Tooltip display |
| `total_k` | `numeric` | G4, total column | Thousands of employed residents | Proportion denominator |
| `bar_extent` | `numeric` | Derived: `emp_rate × prop` | Percent-points, 0–100 | Width of each stacked segment |
| `xmin2` | `numeric` | Derived: cumulative stacking + sign flip | Signed percent, −100 to +100 | Left edge of segment (`geom_rect`) |
| `xmax2` | `numeric` | Derived: cumulative stacking + sign flip | Signed percent, −100 to +100 | Right edge of segment (`geom_rect`) |

**Education level factor definitions** (in stacking order, bottom to top):

| Level | Label | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Below Secondary | No secondary qualification attained |
| 2 | Secondary | GCE N/O-Level or equivalent |
| 3 | Post-Secondary (Non-Tertiary) | ITE, vocational certificates |
| 4 | Diploma & Prof Qual | Polytechnic diploma or professional qualification |
| 5 | Degree | Bachelor's degree or above |

**Sign convention for `xmin2` / `xmax2`:** negative values place the segment on
the Male (left) wing; positive values place it on the Female (right) wing. A
value of `0` is the central age-group spine.

---

## A.2 `emp_pills` — Bar-End Label Table

24 rows · one row per **age group × sex** combination.

| Column | R Type | Source | Unit / Range | Role in Chart |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `age` | `factor` (12 levels) | Derived from `employment_data` | Age band | Y-axis alignment |
| `sex` | `factor` (2 levels) | Derived from `employment_data` | `"Male"` or `"Female"` | Determines label side |
| `emp_rate` | `numeric` | B1, Sheet B1 | Percent, 0–100 | Label text displayed in pill |
| `bar_end` | `numeric` | Derived: `−emp_rate` (Male) / `+emp_rate` (Female) | Signed percent | X-position of pill label anchor |

**Note:** `emp_pills` is a summary of `employment_data` with education collapsed. It
drives only the pill annotation layer (`geom_text`) and is not used for any bar
geometry.
